content: Hey Saturday, I'm planning to remodel our bathroom and I'm thinking about going with a wet room design with a frameless glass shower. We've got a budget of about $15,000 total. A contractor named Mike gave us a quote but I haven't had a chance to review it closely yet. We're hoping to start the work in April. Do you think this budget is realistic for what we're trying to do?

That's an exciting project! For a wet room with a frameless glass shower in that budget range, you're actually in pretty good territory, though it depends on a few variables. Typically, a mid-range wet room setup runs $8,000-$20,000 depending on the square footage, tile quality, plumbing work needed, and whether you're doing any structural changes. Your $15,000 budget is solid for a well-executed wet room, especially if you're keeping it under 100 square feet. Before diving into Mike's quote, I'd suggest checking: the cost breakdown between labor and materials, whether waterproofing and slope adjustment are included (crucial for wet rooms), and if the frameless glass enclosure price is locked in. Some builders underestimate glass panel costs. What's the approximate size of your bathroom, and do you know if any major plumbing or structural work will be needed?

The bathroom is probably around 70 square feet, pretty standard size for a half bath that we're upgrading. We do need new plumbing for the drain in the wet room area since we're converting the layout, and the contractor mentioned something about a slope for drainage. Mike's quote was around $13,200 total which seems reasonable, but he broke it down as: labor $6,500, materials $5,700, and permits/misc $1,000. Should I be concerned about anything specific with a wet room installation?

Mike's quote looks pretty well-structured and the numbers seem reasonable for that scope of work. The labor-to-materials ratio is solid, and importantly, he's accounted for permits which some contractors try to slip in as add-ons later. For a 70 sq ft space with new plumbing, that pricing is competitive. That said, wet rooms have a few critical details that can go wrong if not executed properly. The slope is essential—ideally 1:80 to 1:100 gradient toward the drain—and if it's not done correctly, you'll get water pooling and potential mold issues. Make sure Mike's quote explicitly includes waterproofing membrane installation (that's usually $800-$1,200 of your materials cost) and a pre-slope with the right substrate. Also, with the frameless glass shower, verify the spec—is it 10mm tempered glass, and is installation with silicone seals included? Ask Mike for his process on the shower base too; many wet rooms use a sloped concrete base before tiling. One more thing: since you're converting the layout, has he assessed the current drainage line? Sometimes rerouting adds unexpected costs. What tile are you planning for the floor and walls?

We haven't locked in the tile yet, but we're looking at porcelain options that would be slip-resistant for the wet room floor and maybe something cleaner looking for the walls. Budget-wise, we're thinking of spending around $1,500-$2,000 on tile. Since we want to keep things modern, we're also considering a wall-mounted toilet and vanity to keep the open feel of the wet room. How much would those typically cost, and should that be part of Mike's quote or do we need separate bids?

Your tile budget of $1,500-$2,000 is solid and should cover nice porcelain throughout if you're strategic—high-quality slip-resistant floor tile runs about $3-$6 per sq ft and wall tile is often cheaper at $2-$4 per sq ft. For the wet room aesthetic, matte or textured porcelain is a great choice since it's durable and less slippery than glossy. Now, regarding the wall-mounted toilet and vanity—these should absolutely be part of Mike's scope, but they might not be included in that $13,200 quote he gave. A decent wall-mounted toilet typically runs $600-$1,200, and a modern floating vanity with sink is usually $500-$1,500, plus installation labor which adds another $300-$500. That could push you $1,500-$3,000 over budget if these fixtures aren't already accounted for. I'd recommend reaching out to Mike directly and asking: does his quote include fixture installation, and if not, what's the add-on cost? It's better to clarify now than discover surprises in April. Also, confirm whether your $15,000 total includes these fixtures or if that needs to expand. Have you and Mike discussed the timeline yet—how long is the project estimated to take?

Good point about clarifying the fixtures. Mike estimated it would take about 3-4 weeks, starting mid-April. One more thing I'm wondering about—we want to make sure the wet room is actually functional long-term, not just a trendy installation that falls apart. What are the most common maintenance or durability issues people run into with wet rooms, and how do we prevent them?

That's really smart thinking—wet rooms absolutely need proper upkeep or you'll face problems. The biggest culprits are moisture damage from inadequate waterproofing or ventilation, grout deterioration (especially with water constantly on the floor), and mold in corners or around fixtures. To prevent these: first, make sure your ventilation is top-notch—you'll want a dedicated exhaust fan (at least 50 CFM for a 70 sq ft bathroom), ideally ducted to the outside, not an attic; run it during and after showers. Second, the waterproofing membrane is your insurance policy—a quality one like Schluter or similar should last 10+ years if installed right, but it needs to be sealed properly at seams and around pipes. Third, use epoxy grout instead of standard grout in the wet room floor area; it's pricier ($400-$600 more) but vastly more resistant to moisture damage and mold. Finally, maintenance-wise, squeegee the floor after showers and ensure the drain isn't clogged. Ask Mike if his waterproofing warranty is explicit in the contract—reputable contractors usually offer 5-10 year warranties on their waterproofing work. That 3-4 week timeline is reasonable too, though spring weather can affect drying times. Does Mike have references from other wet room projects he's done?

I should definitely ask Mike for references, that's a good reminder. Before we commit to anything, I want to get a second opinion on his quote though. Should I get another bid from a different contractor, and if so, what should I specifically ask them to price out so the quotes are comparable?

Absolutely getting a second bid is smart, especially for a project this involved. To make sure you're comparing apples to apples, send Mike's quote details to at least one other contractor and ask them to bid on the exact same scope. Here's what you need both quotes to specifically include: labor and materials broken out separately, frameless glass shower specs (thickness, installation method), waterproofing membrane brand and installation, slope/pre-slope work details, porcelain tile layout and cost, new plumbing for drain line including any rerouting needed, permits and inspections, and a timeline. Then, ask each contractor about warranty coverage—specifically on the waterproofing work, which is the most critical. When comparing, don't just look at the total number; scrutinize line items for vague categories like "misc" or "contingency." A well-detailed quote (like Mike's seems to be) shows professionalism. Also, when you call contractors for second bids, confirm they understand this is a wet room conversion with new plumbing, not just cosmetic updates—that'll help them give accurate estimates. One thing to note: if a second quote comes in significantly lower, ask why. Sometimes it means they're cutting corners on waterproofing or labor. Given your April timeline, I'd recommend getting these second bids within the next week or so. Want help creating a comparison checklist for when you get the bids back?

Yeah, a comparison checklist would actually be really helpful. Also, should we be putting down a deposit now if we want to lock in an April start date, or is it too early? What's the typical deposit structure for a project like this?

Great question about timing. Typical deposit structure for a $13,200 project is 25-50% upfront, with the remainder due upon completion or in phases (usually 25% mid-project, 25% at end). For an April start, you're probably fine timing-wise—most contractors book 4-8 weeks out depending on seasonality, so a mid-April start gives you until mid-to-late February to finalize and deposit. Don't rush the deposit though; wait until you've gotten your second bid(s), compared thoroughly, and feel confident about your choice. Also, have a written contract in place before any money changes hands—it should specify start/end dates, payment schedule, what's included/excluded, warranty details, and what happens if there are delays. Here's your comparison checklist: (1) Total price and labor/materials breakdown, (2) Frameless glass specs and cost, (3) Waterproofing membrane brand and labor, (4) Slope/pre-slope details, (5) Plumbing work scope, (6) Tile brand and installation cost, (7) Permit and inspection costs, (8) Warranty terms (especially waterproofing), (9) Estimated timeline, (10) Payment schedule offered, (11) Cleanup and debris removal included?, (12) Any contingency allowance %. This way, you can literally line them up side-by-side. Once you pick a contractor, negotiate the deposit—many will do 30% to lock in your date. Does this feel like enough to move forward with the comparison process?
